faq_items.question: What is your refund policy?
faq_items.answer: We offer a 14-day money-back guarantee on most coaching programs and courses. If you’re not satisfied within the first 14 days of your purchase, contact us and we’ll issue a full refund. After 14 days, refunds are not guaranteed and are reviewed on a case-by-case basis. In these cases, we may offer prorated credits or the option to switch to a different program instead of a cash refund.

faq_items.question: What payment methods do you accept?
faq_items.answer: We accept all major credit and debit cards, as well as PayPal in most regions. All payments are processed securely via our payment provider.

faq_items.question: How do I cancel my coaching subscription?
faq_items.answer: You can cancel your subscription anytime from the Billing section of your account settings. Your access will remain active until the end of your current billing period.

faq_items.question: Can I change my coaching plan later?
faq_items.answer: Yes. You can upgrade or downgrade your coaching plan at any time. Changes take effect at the start of your next billing cycle, unless otherwise specified during checkout.

faq_items.question: How long do I have access to a challenge?
faq_items.answer: You keep lifetime access to the challenge materials once you join. Email reminders run only for the duration of the challenge, but you can revisit the content anytime.

faq_items.question: Do your courses offer lifetime access?
faq_items.answer: Yes. All self-paced courses come with lifetime access, including future updates to the course curriculum.

faq_items.question: What if I am in a different time zone from my coach?
faq_items.answer: Our scheduling tool automatically displays available session times in your local time zone, making it easy to find slots that work for both you and your coach.

faq_items.question: How long does it take to get a response from support?
faq_items.answer: Our support team typically responds within 1–2 business days. Billing and access issues are prioritized.

quiz_questions.text: Which area of your life currently feels the MOST out of balance?
On a typical week, how often do you feel rushed or overwhelmed?
When you think about the next 30 days, what would you MOST like to improve?
How clearly do you feel you can see your current priorities across work, health, and relationships?
Rate your current satisfaction with your WORK / CAREER.
Rate your current satisfaction with your HEALTH & ENERGY.
Rate your current satisfaction with your RELATIONSHIPS & CONNECTIONS.
When you start your workday, what do you usually do first?
How do you prefer to plan your work?
How sensitive are you to interruptions while working?
What is your biggest struggle with productivity right now?
At what time of day do you usually feel your MOST focused?
On a scale from 110, how easy is it for you to wake up in the morning?
If you could design your ideal wake-up time with no constraints, what would it be?
When you hesitate to take action, what usually stops you first?
On a scale from 110, how confident do you feel sharing your ideas with others?

strategies.title: Schedule Weekly Visibility Wins
strategies.description: Maya blocked 15 minutes every Friday to write and send a brief update email summarizing her key wins for the week.

strategies.title: Use a Simple Meeting Script
strategies.description: Before each meeting, she prepared one insight and one question so she always had something specific to contribute.

strategies.title: Keep a Daily Contribution Log
strategies.description: She tracked three meaningful contributions per day to build evidence for her promotion case.

strategies.title: Rewrite Your Professional Story
strategies.description: Alex created a one-page narrative of his career wins to read before interviews and networking calls.

strategies.title: Send One Courageous Outreach Per Day
strategies.description: He committed to one small but bold action daily, like messaging a recruiter or old colleague.

strategies.title: Build a Wins & Stories Bank
strategies.description: Alex listed 1015 success stories using the STAR method to use in interviews and negotiations.

strategies.title: Pre-Commit to Speaking Once Per Meeting
strategies.description: Daniel made a rule that he would contribute at least one idea or update in every meeting he attended.

strategies.title: Create Supportive Prompt Cards
strategies.description: He wrote short prompts like �22Share one observation�22 on sticky notes to keep in view during calls.

strategies.title: Review Wins Immediately After Meetings
strategies.description: Right after each meeting, he listed what went well to reinforce the new habit.

strategies.title: Practice Out Loud in Short Rehearsals
strategies.description: Ravi rehearsed key sections of his presentation in 5-minute blocks instead of trying to memorize everything.

strategies.title: Test with a Safe Audience First
strategies.description: He ran a low-stakes practice session with a trusted colleague to get feedback and reduce anxiety.

strategies.title: Use a Grounding Anchor Before Speaking
strategies.description: Before presenting, Ravi used a brief breathing pattern and a cue phrase to settle his nerves.

strategies.title: Create Clear Theme Days
strategies.description: Lena assigned specific evenings to her side projects so she wasn�27t context-switching every day.

strategies.title: Match Tasks to Energy Levels
strategies.description: She saved creative tasks for her highest-energy windows and used lower-energy time for admin work.

strategies.title: Run a 30-Minute Weekly Review
strategies.description: Every Sunday, Sara reviewed her projects, calendar, and tasks to reset for the week.

strategies.title: Use One Trusted Capture System
strategies.description: She moved all her scattered notes into a single app and simple list structure.

strategies.title: Choose One 90-Day Focus Theme
strategies.description: Nina committed to a single primary focus for 90 days instead of juggling multiple big goals.

strategies.title: Practice Body-Neutral Check-Ins
strategies.description: Jade replaced harsh self-talk with neutral observations about how her body felt and what it needed.
